_template.what: Hierarchical, annotated template for a component behavioral contract. Copy this file, delete every key beginning with an underscore, and keep only the sections your component actually needs.

how_to_read._tier: required = every contract has it | recommended = include unless clearly N/A | optional = include only when _when applies
how_to_read._when: the condition under which an optional section earns its place
how_to_read._guide: one-line instruction on how to fill the section
how_to_read.underscore_keys: Any key starting with _ is guidance for the author and is NOT part of a finished contract. Strip all of them.

golden_rules: Behavioral, not visual: never write colors, sizes, typography, spacing, radii, shadows, z-index, durations, or class names. Those live in Figma/design tokens.
This contract is authoritative over Figma where they differ. Record any divergence in a figma_note.
Lean by default: start from the required core and add optional sections only when the component's behavior demands them.
Cross-reference shared/nested components instead of duplicating them (e.g. 'see the <name> contract').
Use the Extra Props entry to capture real data inputs that are not expressible as Figma variant properties.

_kind._guide: Almost always 'component'. Reserved for future kinds (e.g. 'pattern').

_category._when: the component belongs to a named family that affects how it is read (e.g. 'chart'). Omit for plain components.

name: Component Name

_name._guide: Canonical human name; should match the Figma component/component-set name (note any misspelling in figma.figmaName).

_version._guide: Semantic version of the BEHAVIORAL contract. Bump major on behavior/transition/emit changes, minor on new states/props, patch on clarifications. Visual-only Figma changes do not bump it.

figma._guide: Where this component lives in Figma. nodeId + URL are the minimum. Add the optional keys when useful.
figma.figmaName: (optional) the exact Figma layer name if it differs from name or is misspelled

documentationPage._when: a separate Figma page documents the variant matrix / anatomy / behavior
documentationPage.note: What this documentation node shows (e.g. variant matrix rows/columns).

figma_design_notes: _when: Figma represents the component poorly — typically a static mock of a dynamic/data-driven component (charts).

_guide: List the caveats. State plainly that screenshots are the source of truth for look/proportion and that code/measurements are approximate; call out what is baked vs. data-driven, i.e. what (counts, shapes, labels, ticks) is variable and derived from data rather than fixed.
description: One short paragraph stating, in behavioral terms, what the component is and does. No appearance details.

_description._guide: Purpose + core behavior in 2-5 sentences. Mention authority over Figma only if relevant.

intent._guide: Concrete use cases — when a designer/developer would reach for this component.

props._guide: Behavioral inputs the consumer controls. One entry per prop. Include 'Extra Props' for data inputs not modeled as Figma variants. Use '_note' for provisional prop sets (e.g. charts).
props._note: (optional) Use when the prop set is provisional, e.g. mirrors current Figma properties and is expected to change when wired to real data.

<Enum Prop>.figma_property: (optional) exact Figma variant property name, or null if it has no Figma mapping
<Enum Prop>.description: What this prop changes about behavior.

<Boolean Prop>.description: What toggling this prop does. If it gates a nested element, say so.

<String Prop>.description: What text it supplies and where it is shown.

Extra Props._guide: Catch-all describing real data props required for a complete implementation that are NOT behavioral toggles or Figma variants (image sources, URLs/handlers, item collections, data series, etc.).
Extra Props.description: Additional data props required for implementation but not behavioral toggles: ...
Extra Props.figma_note: (optional) State explicitly where this contract overrides the Figma demo state, and that the contract is authoritative.

modes._when: an external/parent-driven mode (not an internal state) changes how the component behaves (e.g. default vs selectable).
modes._guide: Describe each mode and how it changes the same triggers' effects.
modes.description: How modes are applied (usually by the parent container) and what they change.

<mode-name>.description: What this mode does and how interactions differ in it.

variants._when: one component renders several major variants that differ in content/sections (e.g. Video / Live / Empty State).
variants._guide: One entry per variant: its purpose, header, and which sections it shows. Pair with conditional_behaviors.

<Variant>.header: (optional) header title/icon used by this variant.

sections.<Section>: What this section shows in this variant.

states._when: the component has distinct interactive UI states (rest/hover/selected/disabled/...). Display-only components omit this.
states._guide: One entry per state. figma_variant maps to the Figma variant label or null if inferred. Always give entry/exit conditions.

<state>.description: What is true while in this state.

state_machine._when: the component moves between states or variants and the moves are worth formalizing. This is the SINGLE place for transitions — including variant-to-variant conversions (e.g. Empty State -> Video) — and for named semantic operations (e.g. toggle/activate) reachable from multiple triggers.
state_machine._guide: Declare the initial state and every valid transition (from/to/trigger/guard). Variant conversions are just transitions. Document multi-trigger operations as named actions with their effects and emits.

transitions.trigger: the user uploads a first video

<named_action>.description: A semantic operation (toggle/activate/select) reachable from multiple triggers.

triggers._when: the component is interactive. Covers all runtime interactions, including chart hover/scrub/select. Use input categories (pointer/keyboard/touch/resize/programmatic) for rich components, or a flat set of named triggers for simple ones.
triggers._guide: Each trigger has a description (what fires it) and an effect (what the component does). For interactions Figma mocks statically (charts), add a figma_limitation note on the trigger; record what is data-driven in figma_design_notes.

emits._when: the component fires events outward to its consumer.
emits._guide: Each: description, payload shape, and not_emitted_when suppression rules.

accessibility._when: a11y behavior matters for this component AND the behavioral prompt calls for it. (aria-* may instead be modeled as props — see the template's <String Prop>.)
accessibility._guide: Capture role, managed ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation, focus management, and screen-reader announcements — behaviorally, not visually.

keyboard_navigation.tab_stop: when it is in the tab order

focus_management.focus_visible: when a focus indicator is required
focus_management.focus_on_disable: what happens to focus if disabled while focused

screen_reader: announcement that must occur on a key interaction

responsive._guide: Behavioral/layout changes driven by size — NOT visual reflow. Use width/height + breakpoint_behaviors. Empty breakpoint_behaviors ({}) means no size-driven behavior.

resize.description: How the component responds to available space (often via a Size prop rather than fluid resizing).
resize.height: determined by content | fixed per size | ...

composition._tier: required (the single structural section for every component, charts included)
composition._guide: What the component is built from and what it asks of its parent. Use nested_components for child components (mark shared:true and used_when), nested_elements for internal/structural parts (for charts: axes, grid, bars/curve, tooltip, etc. — describe each part behaviorally, use a 'parts' map for sub-structure, and used_when for prop-gated parts), slots for consumer-fillable slots, and parent_requirements for obligations on the parent.

<Child Component>.used_when: (optional) the prop/state/variant that makes this child appear

figma.URL: (optional) if the child has its own Figma source

<Child Component>.description: What the child contributes. If it has its own contract, say 'see the <name> contract'.

props._note: (optional) inline a nested child's key props when it has no standalone contract

behaviors: _when: a nested child has no standalone contract, or its behavior in this parent's context is non-obvious (e.g. only one state is interactive, what activating it does, what it never decides for itself).
_guide: Prose bullets describing the child's in-context behavioral rules. Behavioral only — no visuals.

<Internal Element>.description: Internal structure, not a consumer-facing slot.

slots._guide: Consumer-fillable child slots. Use {} or a 'None' note when there are no slots.

parent_requirements._when: the parent must provide layout, context, form association, or label association.
parent_requirements.description: What the parent/consumer must provide beyond props.

constraints._guide: The rules that bound behavior. conditional_behaviors is the workhorse (if/then, optionally with and-if). Add mutual_exclusions and required_combinations when they apply.

mutual_exclusions.rule: things that cannot be active at once

scenarios._guide: Always present as a key. Named end-to-end behavioral flows in plain present-tense prose. Each traces one path from a starting condition to an outcome (and what is emitted, or that nothing is). May be {} for the most trivial display-only components, but prefer at least one or two.

notes: _tier: required. A closing prose summary that ties the contract together: the component's essence, the key conditional behaviors, the relationship to shared/nested components, and an explicit statement that visual specifics live in Figma and that this contract is authoritative over Figma where they differ. May be a string or an object with a 'description' key.
